content:
What it actually is, why it exists, when to use it over plain Promises, and how to build something real with it

5 min read
1 hour ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Almost every JavaScript developer can write async and await in front of a function within their first month of learning. Far fewer can explain why it works the way it does — and that gap shows up constantly in interviews and in production bugs. This guide closes that gap completely: what's actually happening under the hood, when to use async/await versus alternatives, and a real, working example.
What Async/Await Actually Is
JavaScript runs on a single thread — it can only do one thing at a time. But operations like network requests, file reads, or timers take real-world time to complete, and JavaScript can’t just freeze while waiting. The solution is the event loop: JavaScript starts a slow operation, moves on to other work, and comes back to handle the result once it’s ready.
async/await is not a new way of doing this — it's a cleaner way of writing code that was already possible with Promises. async marks a function as one that will handle asynchronous work, and await pauses execution within that function (not the whole…
